excluded_defs.name: ValueOrImpl
excluded_defs.reason: Briefly examined to understand the existing monadic pattern; not needed since or_else uses ok() and status() directly

exploration_log.aha_moment: StatusOr already has ok() (to check state), value() (to access value), and status() (to access error). or_else needs two overloads: const& (returns copy of *this if ok) and && (moves *this if ok, moves status for the fallback call).
exploration_log.hindsight: Could have checked if there's an and_then() already (there isn't) to understand whether or_else should be the first monadic operation or part of a series.

key_decisions.decision: Two overloads (const& and &&) for efficient move semantics
key_decisions.reasoning: Following the same pattern as value_or() which has both const& and && overloads; the && overload allows moving the value out instead of copying it

queries.query_text: Add StatusOr::or_else(callable) that invokes callable with Status when StatusOr holds an error, alongside value_or in absl/status/statusor.h

solve_notes: Added two overloads of `or_else` to `StatusOr<T>` in `statusor.h`: a `const&` overload that returns `*this` if OK or invokes the callable with `status()`, and an `&&` overload that moves `*this` if OK or moves the status into the callable. The pattern mirrors `value_or` which also has both value-category overloads.
task_text:
The `StatusOr<T>` class in `status/statusor.h` supports `value()`,
`value_or()`, `ok()`, and `status()`, but lacks monadic operations for
chaining fallible computations. Add `StatusOr<T>::or_else(callable)`
that invokes the callable with the held `absl::Status` when the
`StatusOr` holds an error, returning a new `StatusOr<T>` produced by
the callable. The callable signature should be `StatusOr<T>(absl::Status)`.
This enables error-handling chains such as
`auto r = Compute().or_else([](Status s) { return Fallback(); });`
without nested if-statements.

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set covers only the new or_else method. An AI agent would additionally look at value_or to understand the const&/&& dual-overload pattern (to know it should implement both), and at ok() to confirm its signature. Without seeing these, the agent might implement only one overload or use wrong state-checking API.
